content.text: Je dois consulter le document du vendeur pour vérifier les champs à sanitiser.

content.content:
1	# GitHub Copilot CLI
2	
3	> **Last verified:** 2026-05-07 by @fcamblor on Copilot Individual (free-limited) plan
4	
5	<!--
6	  Read `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md` first; it defines the shape of
7	  this document. If this file disagrees with the contract, the contract
8	  wins.
9	-->
10	
11	## Endpoints
12	
13	_verified: 2026-05-07_
14	
15	| Method | URL | Headers | Timeout | Response Content-Type |
16	|--------|-----|---------|---------|-----------------------|
17	| GET    | `https://api.github.com/copilot_internal/user` | `Authorization: token <oauth>`, `Accept: application/json`, `Editor-Version: <vendor const>`, `Editor-Plugin-Version: <vendor const>`, `User-Agent: <vendor const>`, `X-Github-Api-Version: <vendor const>` | 5s | `application/json` |
18	
19	The four "vendor const" headers are constants in
20	`Connectors/CopilotConstants.swift`; they impersonate the official VS
21	Code Copilot Chat extension closely enough to satisfy the
22	`copilot_internal/user` endpoint.
23	
24	## Credential sources
25	
26	_verified: 2026-05-07_
27	
28	Cascade (read-only — the app never writes any of these):
29	
30	1. `$GITHUB_TOKEN` environment variable.
31	2. macOS Keychain entry `gh:github.com` written by the `gh` CLI. Token
32	   may be raw, hex-encoded with `0x` prefix, or `go-keyring-base64:` +
33	   base64-encoded body — the locator handles all three shapes.
34	3. `gh` `hosts.yml`, searched in this order:
35	   - `$GH_CONFIG_DIR/hosts.yml` if set,
36	   - `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/gh/hosts.yml` if set,
37	   - `~/.config/gh/hosts.yml`.
38	
39	The active GitHub login is read from `hosts.yml` (`github.com.user`).
40	The token is per-user under `github.com.users.<login>.oauth_token` with
41	fallback to a host-level `github.com.oauth_token` for legacy configs.
42	
43	The `gh` CLI owns lifecycle. The locator never calls `SecItemAdd`, never
44	invokes `gh auth login`, never writes any of these files.
45	
46	## Sanitized fields
47	
48	_verified: 2026-05-07_
49	
50	Drives the leakage test in `Tests/Fixtures/copilot-full-payload.json`.
51	
52	| Location | Field path | Redaction style |
53	|----------|-----------|-----------------|
54	| Header   | `Authorization` value | full removal (`<redacted>`) |
55	| Body     | any `*token*` / `*key*` / `*secret*` / `*password*` / `*credential*` key | full removal |
56	| Body     | `analytics_tracking_id` | full removal (vendor-issued opaque ID — telemetry session) |
57	| Body     | `login` | preserved (it IS the public account identity the user shares; not redacted) |
58	| Body     | any field whose value matches `[A-Za-z0-9._+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+` | replace with `<email>` |
59	
60	`login` is the GitHub username — it's the natural per-account identity
61	the rest of the app keys on, and it's already public via every git
62	commit. Redacting it would break the doc's usefulness without any
63	security benefit.
64	
65	## Plan variants observed
66	
67	_verified: 2026-05-07_
68	
69	### Free / limited (verified by tester)
70	
71	```jsonc
72	<!-- captured: 2026-05-07, plan: free_limited_copilot, login: redacted -->
73	{
74	  "login": "<redacted>",
75	  "access_type_sku": "free_limited_copilot",
76	  "analytics_tracking_id": "<redacted>",
77	  "assigned_date": "2026-05-06T14:16:33+02:00",
78	  "can_signup_for_limited": false,
79	  "chat_enabled": true,
80	  "copilotignore_enabled": false,
81	  "copilot_plan": "individual",
82	  "is_mcp_enabled": true,
83	  "organization_login_list": [],
84	  "organization_list": [],
85	  "restricted_telemetry": false,
86	  "endpoints": {
87	    "api": "https://api.individual.githubcopilot.com",
88	    "origin-tracker": "https://origin-tracker.individual.githubcopilot.com",
89	    "proxy": "https://proxy.individual.githubcopilot.com",
90	    "telemetry": "https://telemetry.individual.githubcopilot.com"
91	  },
92	  "limited_user_quotas": {
93	    "chat": 480,
94	    "completions": 4000
95	  },
96	  "limited_user_subscribed_day": 6,
97	  "limited_user_reset_date": "2026-06-06",
98	  "monthly_quotas": {
99	    "chat": 500,
100	    "completions": 4000
101	  }
102	}
103	```
104	
105	`limited_user_quotas` carries the **remaining** count for each pool;
106	`monthly_quotas` carries the **total**. Connector computes
107	`used = total - remaining` and emits a percent.
108	
109	### Pro / paid (assumed, not yet verified by tester)
110	
111	The Pro plan exposes a `quota_snapshots` block with `percent_remaining`
112	fields, plus a single `quota_reset_date`. Expected shape:
113	
114	```jsonc
115	<!-- captured: assumed, plan: copilot_individual_pro, login: redacted -->
116	{
117	  "login": "<redacted>",
118	  "copilot_plan": "individual_pro",
119	  "quota_reset_date": "2026-06-06",
120	  "quota_snapshots": {
121	    "premium_interactions": { "percent_remaining": 73, "unlimited": false },
122	    "chat":                 { "percent_remaining": 100, "unlimited": true }
123	  }
124	}
125	```
126	
127	`unlimited: true` for a pool does NOT cause the metric to be skipped —
128	paid plans expose `premium_interactions` with `unlimited: true` (soft
129	monthly allowance, overage billed separately) where `percent_remaining`
130	remains the meaningful signal.
131	
132	### Business / Enterprise plans (assumed, not yet verified by tester)
133	
134	Likely populate `organization_list` and `organization_login_list` with
135	per-org entitlement records. `endpoints.api` may point at an enterprise
136	proxy. Quota fields shape unknown.
137	
138	## Metric semantics
139	
140	_verified: 2026-05-07_
141	
142	| Swift metric | Source field | Reset cadence | Unit | Edge cases |
143	|--------------|--------------|---------------|------|------------|
144	| `.timeWindow("Premium")` | `quota_snapshots.premium_interactions.percent_remaining` / `quota_reset_date` | calendar monthly | percent | `unlimited: true` is NOT skipped; missing block → metric omitted |
145	| `.timeWindow("Chat")`    | `quota_snapshots.chat.percent_remaining` / `quota_reset_date` | calendar monthly | percent | same |
146	| `.timeWindow("Chat")`    | `(monthly_quotas.chat - limited_user_quotas.chat) / monthly_quotas.chat × 100` / `limited_user_reset_date` | calendar monthly | percent | free tier — emitted only when both blocks present |
147	| `.timeWindow("Completions")` | `(monthly_quotas.completions - limited_user_quotas.completions) / ...` / `limited_user_reset_date` | calendar monthly | percent | free tier |
148	
149	Free-tier "Chat" and paid-tier "Chat" share the metric name on purpose —
150	the UI keys on vendor + name, and a single user is on exactly one tier
151	at a time, so there's no collision.
152	
153	The synthetic 30-day window duration (`monthlyWindowMinutes = 30 * 24 *
154	60`) matches openusage's default — the API exposes only an absolute
155	reset date, not a window length.
156	
157	## Time semantics
158	
159	_verified: 2026-05-07_
160	
161	- Reset values arrive as `yyyy-MM-dd` calendar dates:
162	  `"limited_user_reset_date": "2026-06-06"`.
163	- Connector promotes calendar dates to UTC midnight via
164	  `ISODate.parsingFlexibleDate(_:)`. This matters because the rest of the
165	  app expects a parseable ISO 8601 datetime — without normalization,
166	  calendar-only values would be silently dropped as "missing".
167	
168	## Error catalog
169	
170	_verified: 2026-05-07_
171	
172	| Status   | Connector response |
173	|----------|--------------------|
174	| 200      | parse, emit metrics, refresh `lastKnownMetrics` |
175	| 401, 403 | surface `token_expired` (token expired/revoked or no Copilot entitlement) |
176	| 429      | preserve last-known metrics, mark active, surface `http_429` |
177	| other    | surface `http_<code>`; do not refresh metrics |
178	
179	`identity_unresolved` is logged (no entry written) when the active login

# GitHub Copilot CLI

> **Last verified:** 2026-05-07 by @fcamblor on Copilot Individual (free-limited) plan

<!--
  Read `docs/VENDOR-PLUGIN-CONTRACT.md` first; it defines the shape of
  this document. If this file disagrees with the contract, the contract
  wins.
-->

## Endpoints

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Method | URL | Headers | Timeout | Response Content-Type |
|--------|-----|---------|---------|-----------------------|
| GET    | `https://api.github.com/copilot_internal/user` | `Authorization: token <oauth>`, `Accept: application/json`, `Editor-Version: <vendor const>`, `Editor-Plugin-Version: <vendor const>`, `User-Agent: <vendor const>`, `X-Github-Api-Version: <vendor const>` | 5s | `application/json` |

The four "vendor const" headers are constants in
`Connectors/CopilotConstants.swift`; they impersonate the official VS
Code Copilot Chat extension closely enough to satisfy the
`copilot_internal/user` endpoint.

## Credential sources

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Cascade (read-only — the app never writes any of these):

1. `$GITHUB_TOKEN` environment variable.
2. macOS Keychain entry `gh:github.com` written by the `gh` CLI. Token
   may be raw, hex-encoded with `0x` prefix, or `go-keyring-base64:` +
   base64-encoded body — the locator handles all three shapes.
3. `gh` `hosts.yml`, searched in this order:
   - `$GH_CONFIG_DIR/hosts.yml` if set,
   - `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/gh/hosts.yml` if set,
   - `~/.config/gh/hosts.yml`.

The active GitHub login is read from `hosts.yml` (`github.com.user`).
The token is per-user under `github.com.users.<login>.oauth_token` with
fallback to a host-level `github.com.oauth_token` for legacy configs.

The `gh` CLI owns lifecycle. The locator never calls `SecItemAdd`, never
invokes `gh auth login`, never writes any of these files.

## Sanitized fields

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Drives the leakage test in `Tests/Fixtures/copilot-full-payload.json`.

| Location | Field path | Redaction style |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Header   | `Authorization` value | full removal (`<redacted>`) |
| Body     | any `*token*` / `*key*` / `*secret*` / `*password*` / `*credential*` key | full removal |
| Body     | `analytics_tracking_id` | full removal (vendor-issued opaque ID — telemetry session) |
| Body     | `login` | preserved (it IS the public account identity the user shares; not redacted) |
| Body     | any field whose value matches `[A-Za-z0-9._+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+` | replace with `<email>` |

`login` is the GitHub username — it's the natural per-account identity
the rest of the app keys on, and it's already public via every git
commit. Redacting it would break the doc's usefulness without any
security benefit.

## Plan variants observed

_verified: 2026-05-07_

### Free / limited (verified by tester)

```jsonc
<!-- captured: 2026-05-07, plan: free_limited_copilot, login: redacted -->
{
  "login": "<redacted>",
  "access_type_sku": "free_limited_copilot",
  "analytics_tracking_id": "<redacted>",
  "assigned_date": "2026-05-06T14:16:33+02:00",
  "can_signup_for_limited": false,
  "chat_enabled": true,
  "copilotignore_enabled": false,
  "copilot_plan": "individual",
  "is_mcp_enabled": true,
  "organization_login_list": [],
  "organization_list": [],
  "restricted_telemetry": false,
  "endpoints": {
    "api": "https://api.individual.githubcopilot.com",
    "origin-tracker": "https://origin-tracker.individual.githubcopilot.com",
    "proxy": "https://proxy.individual.githubcopilot.com",
    "telemetry": "https://telemetry.individual.githubcopilot.com"
  },
  "limited_user_quotas": {
    "chat": 480,
    "completions": 4000
  },
  "limited_user_subscribed_day": 6,
  "limited_user_reset_date": "2026-06-06",
  "monthly_quotas": {
    "chat": 500,
    "completions": 4000
  }
}
```

`limited_user_quotas` carries the **remaining** count for each pool;
`monthly_quotas` carries the **total**. Connector computes
`used = total - remaining` and emits a percent.

### Pro / paid (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

The Pro plan exposes a `quota_snapshots` block with `percent_remaining`
fields, plus a single `quota_reset_date`. Expected shape:

```jsonc
<!-- captured: assumed, plan: copilot_individual_pro, login: redacted -->
{
  "login": "<redacted>",
  "copilot_plan": "individual_pro",
  "quota_reset_date": "2026-06-06",
  "quota_snapshots": {
    "premium_interactions": { "percent_remaining": 73, "unlimited": false },
    "chat":                 { "percent_remaining": 100, "unlimited": true }
  }
}
```

`unlimited: true` for a pool does NOT cause the metric to be skipped —
paid plans expose `premium_interactions` with `unlimited: true` (soft
monthly allowance, overage billed separately) where `percent_remaining`
remains the meaningful signal.

### Business / Enterprise plans (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Likely populate `organization_list` and `organization_login_list` with
per-org entitlement records. `endpoints.api` may point at an enterprise
proxy. Quota fields shape unknown.

## Metric semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Swift metric | Source field | Reset cadence | Unit | Edge cases |
|--------------|--------------|---------------|------|------------|
| `.timeWindow("Premium")` | `quota_snapshots.premium_interactions.percent_remaining` / `quota_reset_date` | calendar monthly | percent | `unlimited: true` is NOT skipped; missing block → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Chat")`    | `quota_snapshots.chat.percent_remaining` / `quota_reset_date` | calendar monthly | percent | same |
| `.timeWindow("Chat")`    | `(monthly_quotas.chat - limited_user_quotas.chat) / monthly_quotas.chat × 100` / `limited_user_reset_date` | calendar monthly | percent | free tier — emitted only when both blocks present |
| `.timeWindow("Completions")` | `(monthly_quotas.completions - limited_user_quotas.completions) / ...` / `limited_user_reset_date` | calendar monthly | percent | free tier |

Free-tier "Chat" and paid-tier "Chat" share the metric name on purpose —
the UI keys on vendor + name, and a single user is on exactly one tier
at a time, so there's no collision.

The synthetic 30-day window duration (`monthlyWindowMinutes = 30 * 24 *
60`) matches openusage's default — the API exposes only an absolute
reset date, not a window length.

## Time semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

- Reset values arrive as `yyyy-MM-dd` calendar dates:
  `"limited_user_reset_date": "2026-06-06"`.
- Connector promotes calendar dates to UTC midnight via
  `ISODate.parsingFlexibleDate(_:)`. This matters because the rest of the
  app expects a parseable ISO 8601 datetime — without normalization,
  calendar-only values would be silently dropped as "missing".

## Error catalog

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Status   | Connector response |
|----------|--------------------|
| 200      | parse, emit metrics, refresh `lastKnownMetrics` |
| 401, 403 | surface `token_expired` (token expired/revoked or no Copilot entitlement) |
| 429      | preserve last-known metrics, mark active, surface `http_429` |
| other    | surface `http_<code>`; do not refresh metrics |

`identity_unresolved` is logged (no entry written) when the active login
cannot be determined from any source.

## Known unknowns

- Copilot Pro response shape with active `quota_snapshots`.
- Behavior of `unlimited: true` pools beyond `premium_interactions` —
  whether `percent_remaining` is always meaningful or sometimes a fixed
  100.
- Business / Enterprise plan shapes (org-scoped seats).
- `analytics_tracking_id` rotation cadence — may need re-sanitization
  across snapshots if it stays stable enough to identify the session
  uniquely.

## Source references

- `openusage` Copilot connector — origin of the User-Agent / Editor-Version
  header impersonation pattern (https://github.com/openusage/openusage)
  (retrieved 2026-05-07).
- `gh` CLI source — `hosts.yml` schema and keychain layout
  (https://github.com/cli/cli) (retrieved 2026-05-07).
- Maintainer's own captures during development of `CopilotConnector`.

## Change log

- 2026-05-07 — initial capture: free-limited plan response shape,
  calendar-date reset semantics, three-source token cascade
  (env / keychain / hosts.yml), `go-keyring-base64:` decoding tolerance.

content.content:
# Claude Code

> **Last verified:** 2026-05-07 by @fcamblor on Claude Code Pro plan

## Endpoints

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Method | URL | Headers | Timeout | Response Content-Type |
|--------|-----|---------|---------|-----------------------|
| GET    | `https://api.anthropic.com/api/oauth/usage` | `Authorization: Bearer <oauth>`, `anthropic-beta: oauth-2025-04-20` | 5s | `application/json` |

The `anthropic-beta` value matches the rollout date documented when the
OAuth-app usage endpoint was made available.

## Credential sources

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Cascade (read-only — the app never writes any of these):

1. **macOS Keychain entry** `Claude Code-credentials` written by the
   `claude` CLI. Value is JSON of the form
   `{"claudeAiOauth":{"accessToken":"sk-ant-oat01-...","refreshToken":"...","expiresAt":...}}`.

The `claude` CLI owns the lifecycle. The locator never calls
`SecItemAdd`, never invokes `claude auth`, never writes the file at
`~/.claude.json`.

The active account email is read from `~/.claude.json` (`oauthAccount.emailAddress`).

## Sanitized fields

_verified: 2026-05-07_

Drives the leakage test in `Tests/Fixtures/claude-full-payload.json`.

| Location | Field path | Redaction style |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Header   | `Authorization` value | full removal (`<redacted>`) |
| Header   | `anthropic-beta` value | preserved (no secret) |
| Body     | any `*token*` / `*key*` / `*secret*` / `*password*` / `*credential*` key | full removal |
| Body     | any `*email*` key | full removal |
| Message  | `[A-Za-z0-9._+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+` matches | replace with `<email>` |
| Body     | `request-id` (response header) | preserved (diagnostic, no secret) |

The Claude usage endpoint response itself does not include tokens or
emails — the field-name based default-deny is defensive against future
schema changes.

## Plan variants observed

_verified: 2026-05-07_

### Pro plan (verified by tester)

```jsonc
<!-- captured: 2026-05-07, plan: Pro, login: redacted -->
{
  "five_hour": {
    "utilization": 50.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day": {
    "utilization": 13.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571503+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day_oauth_apps": null,
  "seven_day_opus": null,
  "seven_day_sonnet": {
    "utilization": 2.0,
    "resets_at": "2026-05-12T22:00:00.571510+00:00"
  },
  "seven_day_cowork": null,
  "seven_day_omelette": {
    "utilization": 0.0,
    "resets_at": null
  },
  "tangelo": null,
  "iguana_necktie": null,
  "omelette_promotional": null,
  "extra_usage": {
    "is_enabled": false,
    "monthly_limit": null,
    "used_credits": null,
    "utilization": null,
    "currency": null
  }
}
```

Unknown top-level keys (`seven_day_omelette`, `tangelo`, `iguana_necktie`,
`omelette_promotional`, `extra_usage`) are ignored by design — the API
evolves with experimental fields and the connector only consumes the
windows it knows about.

### Max plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Same shape as Pro with additional `seven_day_opus` block populated
instead of `null`. To be confirmed by a tester running on Max.

### Free plan (assumed, not yet verified by tester)

Reportedly returns the same shape with all `seven_day_*` blocks `null`
and `five_hour.utilization` capped on a tighter quota.

## Metric semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Swift metric | Source field | Reset cadence | Unit | Edge cases |
|--------------|--------------|---------------|------|------------|
| `.timeWindow("5h sessions (all models)")` | `five_hour.utilization` / `five_hour.resets_at` | rolling 5-hour window (300 minutes) | percent | `resets_at: null` → emit with `resetAt: nil`; missing block → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly (all models)")` | `seven_day.utilization` / `seven_day.resets_at` | weekly (10080 minutes) | percent | same |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Sonnet")` | `seven_day_sonnet.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |
| `.timeWindow("Weekly Opus")` | `seven_day_opus.*` | weekly | percent | additive — absent → metric omitted |

Window durations are not returned by the API — they are fixed by
Anthropic's rate-limit policy and hard-coded in the connector
(`sessionWindowMinutes = 300`, `weeklyWindowMinutes = 10080`).

## Time semantics

_verified: 2026-05-07_

- Reset values arrive as ISO 8601 datetimes with sub-second precision:
  `2026-05-07T00:29:59.571486+00:00`.
- Connector strips sub-second precision via `normalizeISO8601(_:)` so
  downstream consumers can rely on the standard formatter without
  enabling fractional-seconds parsing.

## Error catalog

_verified: 2026-05-07_

| Status | Connector response |
|--------|--------------------|
| 200    | parse, emit metrics, refresh `lastKnownMetrics` |
| 401    | surface `token_expired`; no metric refresh |
| 429    | preserve last-known metrics, mark active, surface `http_429` |
| other  | surface `http_<code>`; do not refresh metrics |

A `parse_error` is surfaced if the response cannot be decoded as JSON or
contains no known window block.

## Known unknowns

- Max plan shape with active `seven_day_opus` quota — assumed identical
  to Pro plus a populated Opus block. Pending tester verification.
- Free plan shape — assumed Pro-shaped with reduced quotas. Pending
  tester verification.
- Enterprise / Team plans — completely unverified. Behavior unknown.
- The `extra_usage` block's `is_enabled: true` payload — never observed
  by the maintainer. Pending tester verification.

## Source references

- Anthropic API documentation, OAuth usage endpoint
  (https://docs.anthropic.com/) (retrieved 2026-05-07).
- Maintainer's own captures during development of `ClaudeCodeConnector`.

## Change log

- 2026-05-07 — initial capture: Pro plan response shape, fixed window
  durations, ISO 8601 with sub-second precision, beta header
  `oauth-2025-04-20`.

Now I have all the information needed. Let me analyze:

**Phase A — Verify scope and phase** ✅
- Issue #33: `[new-assistant] GitHub Copilot CLI`
- Labels: `type:new-assistant`, `phase:testing` ✅

**Phase B — Locate the latest build** ✅
- Short SHA: `d5667408`
- Full SHA: `d5667408dafd1697b118db47946bdd23abb1092c`
- PR: #34

**Phase C — Scan for sign-offs**


Found one `tester-confirm` sign-off from @fcamblor (the PR author and owner) with the matching build SHA, Free plan, macOS 26.2, in-app submission path, all checkboxes completed including the optional one, and the connector log included inline in the comment.

The PR author is permitted to sign off as a tester since they're testing the DMG against their own vendor account like any other tester would. The log is in a `<details>` block rather than attached as a file, which is unconventional but still auditable, so I'll count it as valid.

Now I'm checking the sanitization requirements from the vendor documentation to see what fields need redaction in the logs.

Looking at the actual log entries, the `login=fcamblor` field is preserved as-is, which aligns with the vendor spec that explicitly says login is the public account identity and shouldn't be redacted. ✅ The reference to `token from keychain` is just describing where the token came from, not exposing the actual token value itself, so that's also clean. ✅ No Authorization headers, analytics IDs, emails, or credentials are logged anywhere.

For the threshold calculation, I've got 1 valid confirmation so far but need 2 distinct confirmations for the `type:new-assistant` category, so I'm still short by one tester.

Now I need to check if there's already a tally comment I should update, or if I need to create a new one. I don't see an existing one, so I'll compose and post a fresh tally comment that documents the single confirmation from @fcamblor on the latest build, notes that sanitization looks good, and indicates we're waiting for one more tester to meet the threshold. I don't see an existing tally comment on the issue, so I'll need to create a new one to post my findings.

---

- **Build de référence :** `d5667408` (PR #34)
- **Confirmations valides :** 1/2 — @fcamblor (Free, macOS 26.2) — log audité propre
- **Gaps de sanitisation :** aucun
- **Verdict :** ❌ **Seuil non atteint** — 1 tester supplémentaire requis sur le build `d5667408` avec log joint.

Il faut recruter un deuxième testeur (`type:new-assistant` exige ≥ 2 confirmations distinctes). Une fois la deuxième confirmation valide reçue, invoquer `/assistant-tester-followup 33` à nouveau, puis si le seuil est atteint, appliquer `phase:merge-ready` et lancer `/assistant-merge 33`.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/fcamblor/conductor/workspaces/mac-ai-trackers/boston/.claude/skills/assistant-tester-followup

# Assistant tester follow-up

## Prerequisite

Read `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`; it owns the sign-off template, the
counting rules, the threshold table, and the sanitization-audit
discipline. If this skill and the spec disagree, the spec wins.

## Argument

A GitHub issue number.

## Phases

### Phase A — Verify scope and phase

Refuse unless the issue is at `phase:testing`. Read the issue's `type:*`
and (when present) `kind:*` labels — they drive the threshold.

### Phase B — Locate the latest build

Find the most recent `<!-- assistant-build:sticky -->` comment on the
issue. Extract the short SHA and the full SHA. This is the SHA every
counted confirmation must match — older builds are invalidated.

### Phase C — Scan for sign-offs

Iterate through the issue's comments and pick the ones starting with the
`✅ tester-confirm` sentinel. For each, validate:

- the build SHA in the body matches the latest sticky's SHA (both
  forms),
- required Verified boxes are ticked,
- a connector log file is attached (or the comment explicitly states
  verbose mode produced no output — that itself is a bug).

### Phase D — Audit attached logs

For each attached connector log:

- read it (or guide the user to fetch and read it),
- cross-check against the vendor doc's `Sanitized fields` section:
  every listed field MUST be redacted in the log,
- flag obvious secret patterns (long base64-ish strings, tokens,
  emails not declared as public in the vendor doc) as a sanitization
  gap.

A sanitization gap blocks the count and surfaces as a high-priority
finding for the maintainer — the connector code must be fixed and a
fresh DMG built before that confirmation can be re-counted. When
quoting a suspect line in the audit comment, replace the suspected
value with `<redacted>` so the issue thread itself never republishes a
leak.

### Phase E — Compute the threshold

| Issue labels | Threshold |
|---|---|
| `type:new-assistant` | ≥ 2 distinct confirmations |
| `type:vendor-evolution` + `kind:enrichment` | ≥ 2 distinct confirmations |
| `type:vendor-evolution` + `kind:breaking` | ≥ 2 distinct confirmations |
| `type:vendor-evolution` + `kind:urgent-fix` | ≥ 1 confirmation |

### Phase F — Sticky tally comment

Update or create the sticky tally comment on the issue (sentinel
`<!-- assistant-tester-tally:sticky -->`). Body shape:

```
<!-- assistant-tester-tally:sticky -->
Tester confirmations: N/<threshold> ✅

Valid confirmations on the latest build SHA `<short>`:
- @<tester> — Plan: <plan>, macOS: <version>
- ...

Incomplete (please respond):
- @<tester> — Missing: <field>
- ...

Sanitization gaps (block merge):
- @<tester>'s log: line <n> contains a suspected secret
  pattern (`<redacted>` in the value field).
- ...
```

### Phase G — Verdict

- If `N >= threshold` and **no** sanitization gap is open → recommend
  the maintainer apply `phase:merge-ready` and name `assistant-merge`
  as the next skill.
- Otherwise → list what is missing and ask the maintainer to wait /
  unblock. Do **not** apply any phase label yourself.


ARGUMENTS: issue 33

### Submitting feedback

**Recommended:** open the app, click the "🧪 Tester feedback" banner in the popover.

opens this issue in your browser with the comment pre-written + reveals the
connector log in Finder so you can drag-attach it.

**Manual fallback:** copy the sign-off template from `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`,
fill it in, attach the log file from `~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/copilot-usages-connector.log`.

The build sanitizes secrets (tokens, API keys, emails) before writing to the log;
if you spot anything that looks confidential, flag it in your comment without

content.thinking: The user is asking me to modify the constraints so that having 2 checkmarks is never considered sufficient, regardless of whether they appeared on the latest build or not. Let me parse this carefully since they wrote it in French — they want to remove the condition where 2 ✅ would be acceptable, making it a stricter requirement across the board. I think the user is asking to relax the constraint so that 2 checkmarks count toward approval even if they weren't on the latest build — they want confirmations from previous builds to still be valid, not just those from the most recent one. I'm going in circles on the French phrasing here. The core issue is whether they want to relax the SHA-match requirement or add stricter constraints — and I don't have enough context to be sure. I should just ask them directly what they mean rather than keep second-guessing the interpretation.

content.text:
Je veux m'assurer de bien comprendre avant de modifier la spec. Tu as 3 `✅ tester-confirm` au total, mais sur 3 SHA différents (un seul sur le dernier build `673a1f3f`).

Tu souhaites donc **relâcher** la règle pour qu'une confirmation reste valide même si elle a été postée sur un build antérieur, tant qu'on a 2 confirmations distinctes au total ? C'est-à-dire passer de :

> The build SHA must match the latest sticky build comment (a rebase invalidates older confirmations; testers re-confirm on the new build).

À quelque chose comme :

> Confirmations remain valid across rebuilds. Each distinct GitHub account counts once regardless of which build SHA was used, provided the audit of the attached log remains clean.

C'est bien ça que tu veux ? Si oui, il faudra :
1. Mettre à jour `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` (§3.1.5 Counting rules)
2. Mettre à jour `.claude/skills/assistant-tester-followup/SKILL.md` (Phase C)
3. Re-runner le tally sur l'issue 33 (qui passerait alors à 3/2 ✅ → merge-ready)

Confirme et j'applique.

content.content:
1	---
2	name: assistant-tester-followup
3	description: Tally tester sign-off comments on a phase:testing issue, validate them against the latest build SHA, audit any attached connector logs for sanitization gaps, and draft follow-ups for incomplete confirmations. Use whenever a tester comments.
4	model: sonnet
5	---
6	
7	# Assistant tester follow-up
8	
9	## Prerequisite
10	
11	Read `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`; it owns the sign-off template, the
12	counting rules, the threshold table, and the sanitization-audit
13	discipline. If this skill and the spec disagree, the spec wins.
14	
15	## Argument
16	
17	A GitHub issue number.
18	
19	## Phases
20	
21	### Phase A — Verify scope and phase
22	
23	Refuse unless the issue is at `phase:testing`. Read the issue's `type:*`
24	and (when present) `kind:*` labels — they drive the threshold.
25	
26	### Phase B — Locate the latest build
27	
28	Find the most recent `<!-- assistant-build:sticky -->` comment on the
29	issue. Extract the short SHA and the full SHA. This is the SHA every
30	counted confirmation must match — older builds are invalidated.
31	
32	### Phase C — Scan for sign-offs
33	
34	Iterate through the issue's comments and pick the ones starting with the
35	`✅ tester-confirm` sentinel. For each, validate:
36	
37	- the build SHA in the body matches the latest sticky's SHA (both
38	  forms),
39	- required Verified boxes are ticked,
40	- a connector log file is attached (or the comment explicitly states
41	  verbose mode produced no output — that itself is a bug).
42	
43	### Phase D — Audit attached logs
44	
45	For each attached connector log:
46	
47	- read it (or guide the user to fetch and read it),
48	- cross-check against the vendor doc's `Sanitized fields` section:
49	  every listed field MUST be redacted in the log,
50	- flag obvious secret patterns (long base64-ish strings, tokens,
51	  emails not declared as public in the vendor doc) as a sanitization
52	  gap.
53	
54	A sanitization gap blocks the count and surfaces as a high-priority
55	finding for the maintainer — the connector code must be fixed and a
56	fresh DMG built before that confirmation can be re-counted. When

---
name: assistant-tester-followup
description: Tally tester sign-off comments on a phase:testing issue, validate them against the latest build SHA, audit any attached connector logs for sanitization gaps, and draft follow-ups for incomplete confirmations. Use whenever a tester comments.
model: sonnet
---

# Assistant tester follow-up

## Prerequisite

Read `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`; it owns the sign-off template, the
counting rules, the threshold table, and the sanitization-audit
discipline. If this skill and the spec disagree, the spec wins.

## Argument

A GitHub issue number.

## Phases

### Phase A — Verify scope and phase

Refuse unless the issue is at `phase:testing`. Read the issue's `type:*`
and (when present) `kind:*` labels — they drive the threshold.

### Phase B — Locate the latest build

Find the most recent `<!-- assistant-build:sticky -->` comment on the
issue. Extract the short SHA and the full SHA. This is the SHA every
counted confirmation must match — older builds are invalidated.

### Phase C — Scan for sign-offs

Iterate through the issue's comments and pick the ones starting with the
`✅ tester-confirm` sentinel. For each, validate:

- the build SHA in the body matches the latest sticky's SHA (both
  forms),
- required Verified boxes are ticked,
- a connector log file is attached (or the comment explicitly states
  verbose mode produced no output — that itself is a bug).

### Phase D — Audit attached logs

For each attached connector log:

- read it (or guide the user to fetch and read it),
- cross-check against the vendor doc's `Sanitized fields` section:
  every listed field MUST be redacted in the log,
- flag obvious secret patterns (long base64-ish strings, tokens,
  emails not declared as public in the vendor doc) as a sanitization
  gap.

A sanitization gap blocks the count and surfaces as a high-priority
finding for the maintainer — the connector code must be fixed and a
fresh DMG built before that confirmation can be re-counted. When
quoting a suspect line in the audit comment, replace the suspected
value with `<redacted>` so the issue thread itself never republishes a
leak.

### Phase E — Compute the threshold

| Issue labels | Threshold |
|---|---|
| `type:new-assistant` | ≥ 2 distinct confirmations |
| `type:vendor-evolution` + `kind:enrichment` | ≥ 2 distinct confirmations |
| `type:vendor-evolution` + `kind:breaking` | ≥ 2 distinct confirmations |
| `type:vendor-evolution` + `kind:urgent-fix` | ≥ 1 confirmation |

### Phase F — Sticky tally comment

Update or create the sticky tally comment on the issue (sentinel
`<!-- assistant-tester-tally:sticky -->`). Body shape:

```
<!-- assistant-tester-tally:sticky -->
Tester confirmations: N/<threshold> ✅

Valid confirmations on the latest build SHA `<short>`:
- @<tester> — Plan: <plan>, macOS: <version>
- ...

Incomplete (please respond):
- @<tester> — Missing: <field>
- ...

Sanitization gaps (block merge):
- @<tester>'s log: line <n> contains a suspected secret
  pattern (`<redacted>` in the value field).
- ...
```

### Phase G — Verdict

- If `N >= threshold` and **no** sanitization gap is open → recommend
  the maintainer apply `phase:merge-ready` and name `assistant-merge`
  as the next skill.
- Otherwise → list what is missing and ask the maintainer to wait /
  unblock. Do **not** apply any phase label yourself.

# Markdown authoring rules

## Language: English only

All Markdown files in this repository MUST be written in English, regardless of the conversation language. This includes `CLAUDE.md`, every file under `docs/`, `README.md`, and any new `*.md`.

- When creating a new Markdown file, write it in English from the start even if the user's prompt is in another language.
- When editing an existing Markdown file that contains non-English prose, translate the edited sections to English instead of preserving mixed languages.
- Code blocks, file paths, commands, and identifiers keep their original form — this rule applies to natural-language prose only.

## Write for longevity

Markdown in this repository is long-lived context. Optimize each file so it does not need to be rewritten as the code evolves. Avoid anything that ties documentation to a specific moment in time or to renamable artifacts:

- Do NOT reference specific commit SHAs, PR numbers, or branch names.
- Do NOT describe the project as being in a transient state ("currently a Hello World", "not yet implemented", "WIP"). If a feature does not exist, simply do not document it.
- Do NOT speculate about intent or future direction ("the name suggests…", "will eventually…"). Document what the code actually does today in terms that remain true tomorrow.
- Do NOT list every file, symbol, or dependency when a structural description is enough — exhaustive lists drift out of date quickly.
- Avoid naming specific files, types, or symbols that could be renamed or moved. Prefer describing roles and responsibilities ("the SwiftUI entry point", "the package manifest") over fixed identifiers. When a path is truly stable because a tool mandates it (e.g. `Package.swift` for Swift Package Manager, `.claude/rules/` for Claude Code), it is fine to use it.
- Do NOT duplicate information that already lives authoritatively in code or tool manifests (version numbers, platform minimums, target lists). Point readers at the source of truth instead of mirroring it.

When in doubt, ask: "Will this sentence still be accurate after the next three features ship, or after a plausible rename?" If not, rephrase or remove it.

## Single source of truth across Markdown files

When two Markdown files cover the same topic, exactly one is the authoritative spec; the other references it and adds only what is genuinely its own (workflow steps, scaffolding procedure, agent phases, etc.).

This applies in particular to these pairs:

- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (skill that implements it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/agents/<name>.md` (agent prompt that uses it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/commands/<name>.md` (slash command that triggers it)

Rules:

- Templates, file formats, naming conventions, and directory layouts belong in the spec, NOT in the operational artifact.
- The operational artifact MUST start with a one-line pointer ("Read `docs/<topic>.md` first; it defines X, Y, Z.") and MUST include a conflict-resolution line ("If this artifact disagrees with `docs/<topic>.md`, the spec wins.").
- Before adding concrete content (a template block, a format string, a list of sections) to an operational artifact, check whether that content already exists in a `docs/` spec. If it does, reference it instead of copying it.

## Exception: `roadmap/`

Files under `roadmap/` intentionally describe planned, not-yet-implemented work. The "Write for longevity" rules above (no transient state, no speculation about future direction) do NOT apply there — a roadmap is precisely a record of intent and ordering that will evolve as features ship. The English-only rule still applies. See `docs/ROADMAP.md` for the roadmap file conventions.

# Skill / agent / command authoring

Skills, agents, and slash commands are **operational** artifacts: they describe workflow (phases, steps, prompts). They are NOT the place to define domain conventions.

## Delegate specs to `docs/`

If the artifact implements a process that has (or deserves) a spec under `docs/`:

1. Write or extend the spec in `docs/<topic>.md` first. The spec owns: directory layout, naming conventions, file templates, list of required sections, line/format grammars.
2. In the artifact, include near the top:
   - A short "Prerequisite: read `docs/<topic>.md`" pointer, naming what the spec provides.
   - A conflict-resolution line: "If this artifact and `docs/<topic>.md` disagree, the spec wins; flag it to the user."
3. Keep the artifact focused on workflow only: phases (gather / validate / generate / confirm), questions to ask the user, duplicate checks, tool invocations.

## What NOT to copy into an artifact

- Concrete template blocks (```` ```markdown ... ``` ````) that repeat the spec's template.
- Explicit section lists that restate the spec's structure.
- Format strings (e.g. index line grammars, slug grammars) already fixed by the spec.

Reference them by pointing at the spec instead.

## When there is no spec

If the process is too small to deserve a `docs/` spec, keep everything inline in the artifact — but then no spec file should exist for that topic. Do not split a small topic across two files.

---
name: assistant-tester-followup
description: Tally tester sign-off comments on a phase:testing issue, validate them against the latest build SHA, audit any attached connector logs for sanitization gaps, and draft follow-ups for incomplete confirmations. Use whenever a tester comments.
model: sonnet
---

# Assistant tester follow-up

## Prerequisite

Read `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`; it owns the sign-off template, the
counting rules, the threshold table, and the sanitization-audit
discipline. If this skill and the spec disagree, the spec wins.

## Argument

A GitHub issue number.

## Phases

### Phase A — Verify scope and phase

Refuse unless the issue is at `phase:testing`. Read the issue's `type:*`
and (when present) `kind:*` labels — they drive the threshold.

### Phase B — Locate the latest build

Find the most recent `<!-- assistant-build:sticky -->` comment on the
issue. Extract the short SHA and the full SHA. This is the build the
tally references for newcomers; existing confirmations on prior builds
remain valid (cf. `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` Counting rules).

### Phase C — Scan for sign-offs

Iterate through the issue's comments and pick the ones starting with the
`✅ tester-confirm` sentinel. For each, validate:

- the build SHA is present in the body (both forms — recorded for
  traceability, no longer required to match the latest sticky),
- required Verified boxes are ticked,
- a connector log file is attached (or the comment explicitly states
  verbose mode produced no output — that itself is a bug).

### Phase D — Audit attached logs

For each attached connector log:

- read it (or guide the user to fetch and read it),
- cross-check against the vendor doc's `Sanitized fields` section:
  every listed field MUST be redacted in the log,
- flag obvious secret patterns (long base64-ish strings, tokens,
  emails not declared as public in the vendor doc) as a sanitization
  gap.

A sanitization gap blocks the count and surfaces as a high-priority
finding for the maintainer — the connector code must be fixed and a
fresh DMG built before that confirmation can be re-counted. When
quoting a suspect line in the audit comment, replace the suspected
value with `<redacted>` so the issue thread itself never republishes a
leak.

### Phase E — Compute the threshold

| Issue labels | Threshold |
|---|---|
| `type:new-assistant` | ≥ 2 distinct confirmations |
| `type:vendor-evolution` + `kind:enrichment` | ≥ 2 distinct confirmations |
| `type:vendor-evolution` + `kind:breaking` | ≥ 2 distinct confirmations |
| `type:vendor-evolution` + `kind:urgent-fix` | ≥ 1 confirmation |

### Phase F — Sticky tally comment

Update or create the sticky tally comment on the issue (sentinel
`<!-- assistant-tester-tally:sticky -->`). Body shape:

```
<!-- assistant-tester-tally:sticky -->
Tester confirmations: N/<threshold> ✅

Valid confirmations on the latest build SHA `<short>`:
- @<tester> — Plan: <plan>, macOS: <version>
- ...

Incomplete (please respond):
- @<tester> — Missing: <field>
- ...

Sanitization gaps (block merge):
- @<tester>'s log: line <n> contains a suspected secret
  pattern (`<redacted>` in the value field).
- ...
```

### Phase G — Verdict

- If `N >= threshold` and **no** sanitization gap is open → recommend
  the maintainer apply `phase:merge-ready` and name `assistant-merge`
  as the next skill.
- Otherwise → list what is missing and ask the maintainer to wait /
  unblock. Do **not** apply any phase label yourself.

lines: <!-- assistant-tester-tally:sticky -->
Tester confirmations: N/<threshold> ✅
-Valid confirmations on the latest build SHA `<short>`:
-- @<tester> — Plan: <plan>, macOS: <version>
+Latest build SHA: `<short>`
+Valid confirmations:
+- @<tester> — Plan: <plan>, macOS: <version>, build: `<short>`
Incomplete (please respond):

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- animate: Review a feature and enhance it with purposeful animations, micro-interactions, and motion effects that improve usability and delight. Use when the user mentions adding animation, transitions, micro-interactions, motion design, hover effects, or making the UI feel more alive.
- quieter: Tones down visually aggressive or overstimulating designs, reducing intensity while preserving quality. Use when the user mentions too bold, too loud, overwhelming, aggressive, garish, or wants a calmer, more refined aesthetic.
- archon: Use when: User wants to run Archon workflows, CREATE workflows or commands,
set up Archon, or manage Archon configuration.
Triggers (run): "use archon to", "run archon", "archon workflow", "use archon for",
          "have archon", "let archon", "ask archon to".
Triggers (create): "create a workflow", "write a workflow", "make a command",
          "author a workflow", "new workflow", "new command", "archon workflow yaml".
Triggers (setup): "set up archon", "install archon", "how to use archon",
          "configure archon", "archon setup", "get started with archon".
Triggers (config): "change my archon config", "modify archon config", "archon config",
          "change archon settings", "update my config", "help me change my config",
          "edit archon config", "archon configuration".
Triggers (init): "initialize archon", "set up .archon", "archon init", "add archon to repo".
Capability: Runs AI workflows in isolated git worktrees for parallel development.
Also: Creates and manages workflow YAML files, command files, and configuration.
NOT for: Direct Claude Code work - only for delegating to Archon CLI.
- shape: Plan the UX and UI for a feature before writing code. Runs a structured discovery interview, then produces a design brief that guides implementation. Use during the planning phase to establish design direction, constraints, and strategy before any code is written.
- optimize: Diagnoses and fixes UI performance across loading speed, rendering, animations, images, and bundle size. Use when the user mentions slow, laggy, janky, performance, bundle size, load time, or wants a faster, smoother experience.
- adapt: Adapt designs to work across different screen sizes, devices, contexts, or platforms. Implements breakpoints, fluid layouts, and touch targets. Use when the user mentions responsive design, mobile layouts, breakpoints, viewport adaptation, or cross-device compatibility.
- impeccable: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics. Use when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications, or when any design skill requires project context. Call with 'craft' for shape-then-build, 'teach' for design context setup, or 'extract' to pull reusable components and tokens into the design system.
- clarify: Improve unclear UX copy, error messages, microcopy, labels, and instructions to make interfaces easier to understand. Use when the user mentions confusing text, unclear labels, bad error messages, hard-to-follow instructions, or wanting better UX writing.
- layout: Improve layout, spacing, and visual rhythm. Fixes monotonous grids, inconsistent spacing, and weak visual hierarchy. Use when the user mentions layout feeling off, spacing issues, visual hierarchy, crowded UI, alignment problems, or wanting better composition.
- dbg: Debug applications using the dbg CLI debugger. Supports Node.js (V8/CDP), Bun (WebKit/JSC), and native code via LLDB (DAP). Use when: (1) investigating runtime bugs by stepping through code, (2) inspecting variable values at specific execution points, (3) setting breakpoints and conditional breakpoints, (4) evaluating expressions in a paused context, (5) hot-patching code without restarting (JS/TS), (6) debugging test failures by attaching to a running process, (7) debugging C/C++/Rust/Swift with LLDB, (8) any task where understanding runtime behavior requires a debugger. Triggers: "debug this", "set a breakpoint", "step through", "inspect variables", "why is this value wrong", "trace execution", "attach debugger", "runtime error", "segfault", "core dump".
- distill: Strip designs to their essence by removing unnecessary complexity. Great design is simple, powerful, and clean. Use when the user asks to simplify, declutter, reduce noise, remove elements, or make a UI cleaner and more focused.
- delight: Add moments of joy, personality, and unexpected touches that make interfaces memorable and enjoyable to use. Elevates functional to delightful. Use when the user asks to add polish, personality, animations, micro-interactions, delight, or make an interface feel fun or memorable.
- audit: Run technical quality checks across accessibility, performance, theming, responsive design, and anti-patterns. Generates a scored report with P0-P3 severity ratings and actionable plan. Use when the user wants an accessibility check, performance audit, or technical quality review.
- polish: Performs a final quality pass fixing alignment, spacing, consistency, and micro-detail issues before shipping. Use when the user mentions polish, finishing touches, pre-launch review, something looks off, or wants to go from good to great.
- bolder: Amplify safe or boring designs to make them more visually interesting and stimulating. Increases impact while maintaining usability. Use when the user says the design looks bland, generic, too safe, lacks personality, or wants more visual impact and character.
- typeset: Improves typography by fixing font choices, hierarchy, sizing, weight, and readability so text feels intentional. Use when the user mentions fonts, type, readability, text hierarchy, sizing looks off, or wants more polished, intentional typography.
- critique: Evaluate design from a UX perspective, assessing visual hierarchy, information architecture, emotional resonance, cognitive load, and overall quality with quantitative scoring, persona-based testing, automated anti-pattern detection, and actionable feedback. Use when the user asks to review, critique, evaluate, or give feedback on a design or component.
- colorize: Add strategic color to features that are too monochromatic or lack visual interest, making interfaces more engaging and expressive. Use when the user mentions the design looking gray, dull, lacking warmth, needing more color, or wanting a more vibrant or expressive palette.
- overdrive: Pushes interfaces past conventional limits with technically ambitious implementations — shaders, spring physics, scroll-driven reveals, 60fps animations. Use when the user wants to wow, impress, go all-out, or make something that feels extraordinary.
- assistant: Routes to the correct sub-skill of the assistant family based on the issue's type:* and phase:* labels. Use when the user mentions a vendor onboarding or evolution and is unsure which skill to run.
- assistant-review: Review an open assistant PR against the dual-track checklist (dated documentation first, then code), with per-issue-type addenda for new vendors vs evolution. Use when the issue is at phase:review.
- tech-debt: Create a new technical debt entry in the tech-debt/ directory. Use when identifying technical debt to formalize, track, or document for future resolution.
- assistant-release: Officialize a merged assistant change by updating README (new-assistant only), docs/vendors/index, and crediting testers in release notes; close the issue. Use after a tagged release ships the work.
- improve-ai-harness: Analyze review feedbacks or development friction points and suggest improvements to the AI harness (rules, CLAUDE.md, docs, tooling). Use when asked to improve AI rules, after a code review with issues, or to strengthen the AI configuration.
- assistant-implement: Scaffold (new-assistant) or refactor (vendor-evolution), document, test, and open a draft PR. Use when the issue is at phase:approved or phase:implementing and the contributor is starting or resuming work.
- assistant-merge: Verify gates one last time and squash-merge an assistant PR (new-assistant or vendor-evolution). Use when the issue is at phase:merge-ready.
- roadmap-feature: Create a new epic entry in the roadmap/ directory. Use when the user wants to plan a new feature, add an item to the roadmap, or formalize an epic for future implementation.
- assistant-reverify: Re-verify a vendor's API behavior against its docs/vendors/<vendor>.md snapshot, bump dates, append a Change log entry, and either open a doc-only PR (no drift impact) or file a type:vendor-evolution issue (drift forces connector changes). Use when API drift is suspected.
- assistant-triage: Help the maintainer triage a phase:proposed assistant issue (new-assistant or vendor-evolution) — duplicate check, scope sanity, kind:* label application, decision draft. Use when reviewing an incoming proposal.
- assistant-tester-followup: Tally tester sign-off comments on a phase:testing issue, validate them against the latest build SHA, audit any attached connector logs for sanitization gaps, and draft follow-ups for incomplete confirmations. Use whenever a tester comments.
- session-save: Save the current session state — decisions, modified files, current status, and next steps — to a handoff file for later resume.
- learn-from-mistakes: Now that you learnt some new stuff, write detailed specs and reduce ambiguity before handing work...
- commit: Commit les changements du workspace git avec guidelines opinionated (conventional commits détectés, langue détectée, fixup automatique)
- pr-context: Load the full context of a GitHub Pull Request so the user can ask questions about it.
- deep-init:run: Generate comprehensive codebase documentation with nested CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md and architecture docs
- log-4sh-time:log-4sh-time: Interactive workflow to log work time in 4track with semantic task matching. View aggregated work statistics by project, perimeter, and task. Use this skill when the user wants to create a worklog, log hours, track time, record work activities, or analyze time spent on tasks.
- typescript-standards:enforce-ts-standards: Enforce type-safe TypeScript patterns with Zod validation, pattern matching, and discriminated unions to prevent runtime errors. Use when writing TypeScript code, reviewing for type safety, designing data validation flows, or refactoring to eliminate impossible states.
- oss-security-audit:audit-oss-package: Security audit of a third-party package (npm, pip, homebrew, cargo, gem…) before
using it. Fetches its source code, scans for malicious or suspicious patterns
(outbound network calls, telemetry, eval/exec, install hooks, opaque file writes,
supply-chain risks), then recursively audits same-author dependencies found in the
package manifest.
Use when the user says "is this package safe", "audit this dependency",
"check this npm/pip/brew package", "can I trust this library", or asks
whether a package is trustworthy before adding it to a project.
- impeccable:layout: Improve layout, spacing, and visual rhythm. Fixes monotonous grids, inconsistent spacing, and weak visual hierarchy. Use when the user mentions layout feeling off, spacing issues, visual hierarchy, crowded UI, alignment problems, or wanting better composition.

Use when the user asks to harden, make production-ready, handle edge cases, add error states, design empty states, improve onboarding, or fix overflow and i18n issues.

content.thinking:
I'm debating whether to update the skill description in the YAML frontmatter since it references validating against the latest build SHA, which is now outdated. I'll leave it as is for now since the main body content is already fixed and the description isn't critical to update.

Now let me recount the issue 33 validations with the updated rules. Looking at the three contributors: @fcamblor has a clean log and valid SHA, @juherr has a clean log but is missing the Reset date checkbox confirmation (only 2 of 3 required boxes checked), and @fabienfleureau has everything verified. That gives me two valid confirmations and one incomplete, which meets the threshold — I can recommend moving to merge-ready and should update the tally comment.

content.text:
Maintenant je re-applique le tally avec les nouvelles règles. Je relis @juherr de plus près :
- ✅ Active account
- ✅ Usage metric  
- ❌ **Reset date NOT cochée** → confirmation incomplète

@fcamblor (SHA `d5667408`, log propre, toutes cases ✅) et @fabienfleureau (SHA `673a1f3f`, log propre, 3 cases requises ✅) → **2 confirmations valides**.

input.command:
gh api repos/fcamblor/mac-ai-trackers/issues/comments/4401858269 -X PATCH -f body="$(cat <<'EOF'
<!-- assistant-tester-tally:sticky -->
Tester confirmations: 2/2 ✅

Latest build SHA: `673a1f3f`

Valid confirmations:
- @fcamblor — Plan: Free, macOS: 26.2, build: `d5667408`
- @fabienfleureau — Plan: Pro, macOS: 26.4.1, build: `673a1f3f`

Incomplete (please respond):
- @juherr — Missing: Reset date verification checkbox (build: `d93986a5`)

Sanitization gaps (block merge):
(none)

> Note: counting rules updated — confirmations on prior build SHAs remain valid as long as the attached log audits clean. The build SHA is recorded for traceability.
EOF
)" 2>&1 | head -c 300
input.description: Mettre à jour le tally avec les nouvelles règles

---
name: assistant-tester-followup

# Assistant tester follow-up

## Prerequisite

Read `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`; it owns the sign-off template, the
counting rules, the threshold table, and the sanitization-audit
discipline. If this skill and the spec disagree, the spec wins.

## Argument

A GitHub issue number.

## Phases

### Phase A — Verify scope and phase

Refuse unless the issue is at `phase:testing`. Read the issue's `type:*`
and (when present) `kind:*` labels — they drive the threshold.

### Phase B — Locate the latest build

Find the most recent `<!-- assistant-build:sticky -->` comment on the
issue. Extract the short SHA and the full SHA. This is the build the
tally references for newcomers; existing confirmations on prior builds
remain valid (cf. `docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md` Counting rules).

### Phase C — Scan for sign-offs

Iterate through the issue's comments and pick the ones starting with the
`✅ tester-confirm` sentinel. For each, validate:

- the build SHA is present in the body (both forms — recorded for
  traceability, no longer required to match the latest sticky),
- required Verified boxes are ticked,
- a connector log file is attached (or the comment explicitly states
  verbose mode produced no output — that itself is a bug).

### Phase D — Audit attached logs

For each attached connector log:

- read it (or guide the user to fetch and read it),
- cross-check against the vendor doc's `Sanitized fields` section:
  every listed field MUST be redacted in the log,
- flag obvious secret patterns (long base64-ish strings, tokens,
  emails not declared as public in the vendor doc) as a sanitization
  gap.

A sanitization gap blocks the count and surfaces as a high-priority
finding for the maintainer — the connector code must be fixed and a
fresh DMG built before that confirmation can be re-counted. When
quoting a suspect line in the audit comment, replace the suspected
value with `<redacted>` so the issue thread itself never republishes a
leak.

### Phase E — Compute the threshold

| Issue labels | Threshold |
|---|---|
| `type:new-assistant` | ≥ 2 distinct confirmations |
| `type:vendor-evolution` + `kind:enrichment` | ≥ 2 distinct confirmations |
| `type:vendor-evolution` + `kind:breaking` | ≥ 2 distinct confirmations |
| `type:vendor-evolution` + `kind:urgent-fix` | ≥ 1 confirmation |

### Phase F — Sticky tally comment

Update or create the sticky tally comment on the issue (sentinel
`<!-- assistant-tester-tally:sticky -->`). Body shape:

```
<!-- assistant-tester-tally:sticky -->
Tester confirmations: N/<threshold> ✅

Latest build SHA: `<short>`

Valid confirmations:
- @<tester> — Plan: <plan>, macOS: <version>, build: `<short>`
- ...

Incomplete (please respond):
- @<tester> — Missing: <field>
- ...

Sanitization gaps (block merge):
- @<tester>'s log: line <n> contains a suspected secret
  pattern (`<redacted>` in the value field).
- ...
```

### Phase G — Verdict

- If `N >= threshold` and **no** sanitization gap is open → recommend
  the maintainer apply `phase:merge-ready` and name `assistant-merge`
  as the next skill.
- Otherwise → list what is missing and ask the maintainer to wait /
  unblock. Do **not** apply any phase label yourself.

1. **`docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`** — règle de comptage relâchée : les confirmations sur builds antérieurs restent valides tant que l'audit du log est propre.
2. **`.claude/skills/assistant-tester-followup/SKILL.md`** — Phase B/C/F mises à jour, description YAML corrigée.
3. **Issue #33 tally** — passe à **2/2 ✅** (@fcamblor + @fabienfleureau valides ; @juherr incomplet — case "Reset date" non cochée).

**Issue #33 — verdict final :** seuil atteint, aucun gap de sanitisation → **prêt pour `phase:merge-ready`**. Tu peux appliquer le label puis lancer `/assistant-merge 33`.

Note : @juherr reste listé comme incomplet (pas pour le SHA, mais parce que la case "Reset date displayed in the popover matches the vendor's reported reset" n'a pas été cochée). Si tu veux compter sa confirmation, il faudrait qu'il poste un follow-up confirmant ce point — mais ce n'est pas bloquant puisque le seuil est déjà atteint à 2/2.

# Smart commit

Tu dois committer les changements git en cours dans le repo courant en suivant les guidelines ci-dessous.

## Contexte utilisateur

Intent fourni par l'utilisateur (peut être vide) : ``

## Étapes

### 1. Analyser l'état du repo

Lance en parallèle :
- `git status` (sans `-uall`)
- `git diff --staged` et `git diff` pour voir les changements
- `git log -n 5 --pretty=format:'%h %s%n%b%n---'` pour voir les 4-5 derniers commits

### 2. Détecter les conventions du projet

À partir des 4-5 derniers commits :
- **Conventional commits ?** Si la majorité suit le format `type(scope): message` (feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, perf, build, ci...) → utilise ce format. Sinon → format libre.
- **Langue ?** Détecte la langue dominante des messages (français, anglais, autre) → utilise la même langue. En cas de doute, français.
- **Style ?** Impératif vs descriptif, majuscule initiale ou non, présence de scope, etc. → imite.

### 3. Détecter un éventuel fixup

Si l'intent utilisateur mentionne un correctif (mots-clés : "fix", "corrige", "répare", "bug", "issue", "problème", "régression", "broken"...) :

1. Identifie les fichiers modifiés (`git diff --name-only HEAD` + fichiers staged).
2. Pour chaque fichier, regarde `git log -n 10 --oneline -- <fichier>` pour voir quels commits récents l'ont touché.
3. Si **un seul commit récent** (dans les ~5 derniers, non encore pushé sur main/master partagé — vérifie avec `git log @{u}..HEAD` si upstream) est clairement à l'origine du code cassé, propose un **`git commit --fixup=<sha>`** au lieu d'un commit classique.
4. Si ambigu ou si le commit date d'avant `@{u}` (déjà pushé en amont partagé), reste sur un commit classique.

Ne fais **pas** de rebase autosquash automatiquement — laisse ce soin à l'utilisateur.

### 4. Rédiger le message

Règles impératives :
- **Le message explique le POURQUOI**, pas le QUOI. Le diff dit déjà ce qui change. Le message dit pourquoi ce changement existe (motivation, contrainte, problème résolu, contexte métier).
- Sujet court (≤72 caractères), en suivant la convention détectée.
- Si le pourquoi ne tient pas dans le sujet, ajoute un corps (ligne vide + explication). Sinon, pas de corps inutile.
- Pas de name-dropping de fichiers/fonctions dans le sujet — le diff suffit.
- Pas de phrases vides genre « Mise à jour du code » ou « Divers fixes ».

Si l'intent utilisateur est vide ou trop vague pour déduire le pourquoi, et que le diff ne le rend pas évident, **demande à l'utilisateur** avant de committer plutôt que d'inventer.

### 5. Staging et commit

- Si rien n'est staged, stage les fichiers pertinents **par nom** (jamais `git add -A` ni `git add .` pour éviter d'embarquer secrets/binaires).
- Ignore les fichiers suspects (`.env`, `credentials*`, clés privées, dumps volumineux). Préviens l'utilisateur si tu en vois.
- Crée le commit via HEREDOC pour préserver le formatage multiligne.
- **Ne jamais** utiliser `--no-verify`, `--amend` (sauf demande explicite), ni `--no-gpg-sign`.
- Si un hook pre-commit échoue : diagnostique la cause, corrige, re-stage, et crée un **nouveau** commit (pas d'amend).

### 6. Vérification finale

Après commit, lance `git status` et `git log -1` pour confirmer. Reporte brièvement : sha court + sujet + (fixup le cas échéant).

## Garde-fous

- Ne push jamais.
- Ne crée pas de commit vide.
- Ne touche pas à `git config`.
- Si plusieurs "unités logiques" distinctes sont mélangées dans le diff, préviens l'utilisateur et propose de splitter plutôt que de tout entasser. Tu peux utiliser `git stash` (avec `-u`/`--keep-index` si pertinent) pour isoler des sous-ensembles — mais cette commande n'est **pas** auto-approuvée, l'utilisateur devra confirmer à chaque usage : ne l'utilise que si c'est vraiment nécessaire.

Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.

- typeset: Improves typography by fixing font choices, hierarchy, sizing, weight, and readability so text feels intentional. Use when the user mentions fonts, type, readability, text hierarchy, sizing looks off, or wants more polished, intentional typography.
- critique: Evaluate design from a UX perspective, assessing visual hierarchy, information architecture, emotional resonance, cognitive load, and overall quality with quantitative scoring, persona-based testing, automated anti-pattern detection, and actionable feedback. Use when the user asks to review, critique, evaluate, or give feedback on a design or component.
- colorize: Add strategic color to features that are too monochromatic or lack visual interest, making interfaces more engaging and expressive. Use when the user mentions the design looking gray, dull, lacking warmth, needing more color, or wanting a more vibrant or expressive palette.
- overdrive: Pushes interfaces past conventional limits with technically ambitious implementations — shaders, spring physics, scroll-driven reveals, 60fps animations. Use when the user wants to wow, impress, go all-out, or make something that feels extraordinary.
- assistant: Routes to the correct sub-skill of the assistant family based on the issue's type:* and phase:* labels. Use when the user mentions a vendor onboarding or evolution and is unsure which skill to run.
- assistant-review: Review an open assistant PR against the dual-track checklist (dated documentation first, then code), with per-issue-type addenda for new vendors vs evolution. Use when the issue is at phase:review.
- tech-debt: Create a new technical debt entry in the tech-debt/ directory. Use when identifying technical debt to formalize, track, or document for future resolution.
- assistant-release: Officialize a merged assistant change by updating README (new-assistant only), docs/vendors/index, and crediting testers in release notes; close the issue. Use after a tagged release ships the work.
- improve-ai-harness: Analyze review feedbacks or development friction points and suggest improvements to the AI harness (rules, CLAUDE.md, docs, tooling). Use when asked to improve AI rules, after a code review with issues, or to strengthen the AI configuration.
- assistant-implement: Scaffold (new-assistant) or refactor (vendor-evolution), document, test, and open a draft PR. Use when the issue is at phase:approved or phase:implementing and the contributor is starting or resuming work.
- assistant-merge: Verify gates one last time and squash-merge an assistant PR (new-assistant or vendor-evolution). Use when the issue is at phase:merge-ready.
- roadmap-feature: Create a new epic entry in the roadmap/ directory. Use when the user wants to plan a new feature, add an item to the roadmap, or formalize an epic for future implementation.
- assistant-reverify: Re-verify a vendor's API behavior against its docs/vendors/<vendor>.md snapshot, bump dates, append a Change log entry, and either open a doc-only PR (no drift impact) or file a type:vendor-evolution issue (drift forces connector changes). Use when API drift is suspected.
- assistant-triage: Help the maintainer triage a phase:proposed assistant issue (new-assistant or vendor-evolution) — duplicate check, scope sanity, kind:* label application, decision draft. Use when reviewing an incoming proposal.
- assistant-tester-followup: Tally tester sign-off comments on a phase:testing issue, audit any attached connector logs for sanitization gaps, and draft follow-ups for incomplete confirmations. Use whenever a tester comments.
- session-save: Save the current session state — decisions, modified files, current status, and next steps — to a handoff file for later resume.
- learn-from-mistakes: Now that you learnt some new stuff, write detailed specs and reduce ambiguity before handing work...
- commit: Commit les changements du workspace git avec guidelines opinionated (conventional commits détectés, langue détectée, fixup automatique)
- pr-context: Load the full context of a GitHub Pull Request so the user can ask questions about it.
- deep-init:run: Generate comprehensive codebase documentation with nested CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md and architecture docs
- log-4sh-time:log-4sh-time: Interactive workflow to log work time in 4track with semantic task matching. View aggregated work statistics by project, perimeter, and task. Use this skill when the user wants to create a worklog, log hours, track time, record work activities, or analyze time spent on tasks.
- typescript-standards:enforce-ts-standards: Enforce type-safe TypeScript patterns with Zod validation, pattern matching, and discriminated unions to prevent runtime errors. Use when writing TypeScript code, reviewing for type safety, designing data validation flows, or refactoring to eliminate impossible states.
- oss-security-audit:audit-oss-package: Security audit of a third-party package (npm, pip, homebrew, cargo, gem…) before
using it. Fetches its source code, scans for malicious or suspicious patterns
(outbound network calls, telemetry, eval/exec, install hooks, opaque file writes,
supply-chain risks), then recursively audits same-author dependencies found in the
package manifest.
Use when the user says "is this package safe", "audit this dependency",
"check this npm/pip/brew package", "can I trust this library", or asks
whether a package is trustworthy before adding it to a project.
- impeccable:distill: Strip designs to their essence by removing unnecessary complexity. Great design is simple, powerful, and clean. Use when the user asks to simplify, declutter, reduce noise, remove elements, or make a UI cleaner and more focused.
- impeccable:animate: Review a feature and enhance it with purposeful animations, micro-interactions, and motion effects that improve usability and delight. Use when the user mentions adding animation, transitions, micro-interactions, motion design, hover effects, or making the UI feel more alive.
- impeccable:optimize: Diagnoses and fixes UI performance across loading speed, rendering, animations, images, and bundle size. Use when the user mentions slow, laggy, janky, performance, bundle size, load time, or wants a faster, smoother experience.

- impeccable:quieter: Tones down visually aggressive or overstimulating designs, reducing intensity while preserving quality. Use when the user mentions too bold, too loud, overwhelming, aggressive, garish, or wants a calmer, more refined aesthetic.
- impeccable:delight: Add moments of joy, personality, and unexpected touches that make interfaces memorable and enjoyable to use. Elevates functional to delightful. Use when the user asks to add polish, personality, animations, micro-interactions, delight, or make an interface feel fun or memorable.

Use when the user asks to review, critique, evaluate, or give feedback on a design or component.

- impeccable:harden: Make interfaces production-ready: error handling, empty states, onboarding flows, i18n, text overflow, and edge case management. - impeccable:polish: Performs a final quality pass fixing alignment, spacing, consistency, and micro-detail issues before shipping. Use when the user mentions polish, finishing touches, pre-launch review, something looks off, or wants to go from good to great.
- impeccable:colorize: Add strategic color to features that are too monochromatic or lack visual interest, making interfaces more engaging and expressive. Use when the user mentions the design looking gray, dull, lacking warmth, needing more color, or wanting a more vibrant or expressive palette.
- impeccable:typeset: Improves typography by fixing font choices, hierarchy, sizing, weight, and readability so text feels intentional. Use when the user mentions fonts, type, readability, text hierarchy, sizing looks off, or wants more polished, intentional typography.
- impeccable:adapt: Adapt designs to work across different screen sizes, devices, contexts, or platforms. Implements breakpoints, fluid layouts, and touch targets. Use when the user mentions responsive design, mobile layouts, breakpoints, viewport adaptation, or cross-device compatibility.
- impeccable:impeccable: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics. Use when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications, or when any design skill requires project context. Call with 'craft' for shape-then-build, 'teach' for design context setup, or 'extract' to pull reusable components and tokens into the design system.
- impeccable:clarify: Improve unclear UX copy, error messages, microcopy, labels, and instructions to make interfaces easier to understand. Use when the user mentions confusing text, unclear labels, bad error messages, hard-to-follow instructions, or wanting better UX writing.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
673a1f3 feat(copilot): support GitHub Copilot CLI usage tracking
Restore the Copilot CLI vendor plugin (connector, credential locator,
active-account monitor, payload sanitizer, plugin factory, fixture, and
tests) previously removed by 5309b96 in preparation for the formal
onboarding workflow.

Re-wire the registrations in AppDelegate (poller, monitor, plugin
register), App/VendorBranding resolver, Loggers (per-vendor file
logger), and the registry conformance test.

Vendor doc (docs/vendors/copilot.md) was preserved across the prior
removal and is reused as-is — research dated 2026-05-07 already covers
endpoints, credential cascade, sanitization rules, plan variants,
metric semantics, time semantics, and error catalog.

Display name and tint hex aligned with issue #33: "GitHub Copilot CLI"
(was "Copilot CLI") and 6E40C9 (was 24292F) — the latter is the
Copilot brand purple rather than GitHub neutral grey.

Closes #33

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: 517033ae15d9

---
79e6ed8 docs(assistant): allow PR author to sign off as a tester
Remove the "non-author tester" restriction. The PR author can now post a valid
✅ tester-confirm sign-off and have it counted toward the merge threshold (≥2
distinct testers for new-assistant/enrichment/breaking, ≥1 for urgent-fix).

The author exercises the same testing workflow as any other tester — building
the DMG, running it against their vendor account, and verifying correctness.
The only difference is that they also authored the code. Nothing in the sign-off
template or audit discipline changes; the change is purely in the counting rule.

Update the spec (`docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md`), the operational artifacts
(assistant-tester-followup and assistant-merge skills), the review checklist,
issue templates, and README.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: 35fd8933f197

---
08790be ci(assistant-build): show build timestamp in sticky comment
The sticky is rewritten on every commit pushed to the PR, so testers had no way
to tell whether the comment they were reading was 5 minutes or 5 days old —
which matters because they pin the build SHA in their sign-off and may rebuild
locally if the DMG is stale.

Add an ISO 8601 UTC timestamp (minute precision) right under the heading so the
freshness signal is unambiguous at a glance.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: 539e3b9e1209

---
b757a6e ci(assistant-build): surface build-from-source alternative next to the download
The build-from-source link was buried at the bottom of the sticky and framed
defensively ("Don't trust this DMG?"), making it look like a fallback for
paranoid testers rather than a first-class option. Move it directly under the
download bullet and reword neutrally so testers see both paths side by side
and pick whichever fits their setup.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: bb2c889407ee

---
0f74218 fixup! fix: improve tester feedback UX and preserve account state on transient errors
Entire-Checkpoint: 95155bcbea56

---

docs(assistant): allow tester confirmations to span multiple builds

Relax the build SHA validation in the counting rules. Confirmations on
prior build SHAs remain valid for the threshold calculation as long as
the attached log audits clean against the vendor doc's Sanitized fields.

The build SHA is still required for traceability (the follow-up skill
records which SHA each tester exercised) but is no longer a blocker to
merging. This accommodates testing workflows where the PR receives
multiple updates between tester sign-offs without forcing each tester to
re-verify on the latest build.

Update the spec (docs/ASSISTANT-ONBOARDING.md), the operational skill
(.claude/skills/assistant-tester-followup/SKILL.md), and correct the
skill description.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
